transcription:
You are a careful and thorough archivist transcribing the content from a fragile archival object. This is the only time that anyone will see this object before it is preserved for posterity and so the description must be as accurate as possible.
Carefully transcribe each line of text in a natural reading order from the attached image. Keep each line separate in the response and ensure that each line ends with a space character.
Do not transcribe letterheads, headers, footers, page numbers, sidebars, or any non-content elements. If there is a written address, such as from a letter or postcard, then it should appear first in the transcription.
Do not transcribe text that is partially visible but not written on the object itself. For example if the object is a folder, and bits of text are visible from documents within the folder, then do not transcribe words from those documents.
Do not transcribe text that is partially visible but written on the opposite side of the page, called "bleed-through". This may appear like mirror writing.
If there is text in multiple directions, then keep each block of text separate in the response, separated by blank lines.
Do not include any additional information or context in the response, only the transcribed text.
If there isn't any recognizable text in the image, return the string "VLM-NO-TEXT".

{ADDITIONAL_CONTEXT}
segmentation:
Carefully look at these {input_length} images and group related sequential images together, in order, into distinct documents or objects.
The first image is {first_input} and the last image is {last_input}.

Group images that logically belong to the same letter, postcard, folder, document, book, manuscript, artwork, or other physical object based on:
- Visual similarity and style
- Content relationship (same document pages, same artwork views, front and back of the same object, etc.
- Sequential nature (consecutive pages, multiple angles of same object)
- Similar formatting, layout, or presentation

A letter and its envelope are considered to belong to the same object.

Objects are NEVER split across multiple distinct groups of sequential images.

Output according to this JSON schema:

{{
  "groups": [
    {{
      "images": ["image_0000.jpg", "image_0001.jpg"],
      "justification": "Explanation for why these images belong together"
    }}
  ]
}}

Requirements:
- Return ONLY the JSON according to the above schema without any text before or after it
- You MUST assign every image to EXACTLY one group.
- You MUST NOT assign an image to more than- You MUST NOT assign an image to more than- You MUST NOT ass set of input images to a group
- Images within a group must have consecutive/sequential numbers (e.g., image_0001.jpg, image_0002.jpg, image_0003.jpg)
- Never group non-sequential images together (e.g., do NOT group image_0001.jpg, image_0002.jpg, image_0005.jpg while leaving image_0003.jpg and image_0004.jpg in a different group)
- Provide clear justification for each grouping decision
- Create as many or as few groups as logically make sense
- Groups can contain a single image if it's truly distinct

Return only valid JSON matching the schema above.
